title: Somali Pirates Are Back — But the Coalition That Beat Them Isn’t Coming
gist: Somali piracy is re-emerging in the Western Indian Ocean, but the 2011 multinational naval coalition that suppressed it cannot be rebuilt because NATO, the US, and European navies are committed elsewhere; the author argues for a regionally-anchored architecture enabled rather than led by external partners.

insights.claim: For the first time in a decade, Somali pirates have hijacked three merchant vessels — the tanker Honour 25, the cargo vessel Sward, and the tanker Eureka — and are holding them off Puntland for ransom.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Signals renewed insurance, rerouting, and armed-guard cost pressure for shipping transiting the Western Indian Ocean.
insights.locator: For the first time in a decade

insights.claim: Since April 21, 2026, the French Navy's Maritime Information Cooperation and Awareness Center recorded seven suspicious activities, five attacks, and four attempted boardings off the Horn of Africa.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Quantifies the tempo of the resurgence for anyone pricing maritime risk.

insights.claim: Somali pirate tactics are unchanged from 2011: an armed skiff seizes a dhow to use as a mothership, pushes out to major shipping routes, boards a poorly protected high-value vessel, and holds it at anchor off Puntland during ransom negotiations.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Defenses that worked in 2011 (hardening, armed guards, corridor transit) remain the relevant playbook.

insights.claim: As the Indian Ocean monsoon continues through September, pirate incidents will likely concentrate in the Gulf of Aden in the coming months.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Seasonal geographic risk window for routing decisions.

themes.theme: Why the 2011 coalition will not return

insights.claim: The 2011 counter-piracy model cannot be rebuilt because NATO has refocused on territorial defense since Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, the US has pivoted to the Indo-Pacific and is consumed by Iran war fallout, and European navies cannot spare assets.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Any counter-piracy response will be thinner and less predictable, keeping residual shipping risk elevated.

insights.claim: NATO's Operation Ocean Shield ended years ago and a restart is highly unlikely, while Operation Atalanta could struggle to compete for ships and aircraft against higher European priorities.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Do not expect large standing multinational patrols to reappear as a backstop.

insights.claim: With US planners consumed by tensions around the Strait of Hormuz, Iran, and Israel, sustained US intelligence, naval, and air contributions to Western Indian Ocean counter-piracy are far less likely than in 2011.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: US enablers, not warships, are the most that regional partners can count on.

insights.claim: At the 2011 peak, Somali pirates were simultaneously holding 32 vessels and 736 hostages while cashing million-dollar ransoms.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Benchmark for how severe an unchecked resurgence could become.
insights.locator: Within a year, pirates were simultaneously

insights.claim: Somali piracy fell from 237 incidents in 2011 to almost none by 2016 after a sustained multinational effort made attacks too risky and unprofitable.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Shows the threat is suppressible but only with sustained, expensive effort now unavailable.

insights.claim: Open-source estimates put coalition counter-piracy operations at roughly $1–2 billion per year at the peak, while piracy's total cost to the shipping industry ran roughly $6–7 billion a year.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Frames the scale of economic damage a full resurgence could re-impose on global shipping.
insights.locator: Open-source estimates put the annual cost

insights.claim: By 2013, private armed guards were aboard an estimated 35 to 40 percent of the roughly 65,922 merchant vessels transiting the high-risk area, adding cost on top of rerouting, higher fuel burn, and steeper insurance premiums.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Private maritime security demand scaled sharply with the threat and could do so again.

insights.claim: Pirates did not abandon the trade from lack of interest; criminal networks stopped investing only because piracy became too risky and unprofitable, and by 2016 shifted to smuggling arms, drugs, and migrants for easier returns.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Piracy is a rational business that returns whenever the risk-reward flips back — the driver to monitor.
insights.locator: But the pirates did not abandon trade

insights.claim: More than 81 percent of Somalis work in the informal sector, Somalia ranks as the most corrupt country in the world, and over two million people in Puntland and Somaliland face extreme drought and food shortages — the onshore conditions that feed piracy remain intact.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: The root economic desperation that makes piracy a repeatable business model has not been addressed.

insights.claim: In April 2024 a reported $5 million ransom freed the MV Abdullah and its 23 crew after 32 days in captivity, showing piracy can still pay millions.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Confirms the payoff structure that sustains investor financing of pirate groups is still live.

insights.claim: If Iran war-related disruption has reduced arms, narcotics, and migrant smuggling flows through the Arabian Sea and Gulf of Aden, some Somali criminal networks may turn back to piracy as an alternative revenue stream.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: A non-obvious second-order link: regional war can push criminal capital back into piracy.

insights.claim: The author argues counter-piracy should shift to a regionally-anchored architecture with Somalia and Puntland supported by capable maritime actors such as India, Oman, the UAE, Kenya, Seychelles, and Djibouti, enabled rather than substituted by external partners.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Points to where new demand for ISR, patrol vessels, and information-sharing platforms will flow.
insights.locator: First, the effort should be regionally anchored

insights.claim: Without a legal back end for arrest, detention, and prosecution, interdiction is only temporary — in past operations naval forces had to release captured pirates because no state would take custody.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: The binding constraint on any regional model is legal capacity, not just patrol hulls.
insights.locator: The response will also require credible legal

insights.claim: In May 2026 an Indian Navy helicopter forced a pirate skiff to jettison its weapons and boarding ladder at sea, demonstrating regional assets can step in where the 2011 coalition no longer operates.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Early proof that India and littoral navies, not Western coalitions, will be the responders to watch.
insights.locator: In May of this year, an Indian Navy helicopter

insights.claim: Maritime measures can suppress piracy but only reforms ashore can prevent its return; a strategy lacking credible governance and livelihood components manages risk at sea rather than dismantling the system that sustains it.
insights.speaker: Benoit Lefevre
insights.so_what: Implies piracy risk will remain structurally recurring absent unlikely Somali governance gains.
